text.title: Latency vs Quality Decision Grid Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for a per-call-site decision grid covering latency, quality, model, and cost.

rule.statement: Every call site has an explicit latency SLO (p50, p95 ms) AND quality floor (eval score) — both measured, not guessed.
rule.rationale: Without both, the team optimises whatever the loudest stakeholder cares about that week.

rule.statement: Baseline measurements (latency + quality + cost) per call site are captured BEFORE any model swap proposal.
rule.rationale: Choosing without a baseline guarantees the team can never tell whether the change helped.

rule.statement: The grid is a two-axis table: rows = call sites; columns = {latency_p50, latency_p95, quality_floor, model, tactic, cost_per_call, owner, last_reviewed}. No fewer columns; additional columns OK.
rule.rationale: Fewer columns drops a dimension and decisions become incomparable; the canonical 8 cover what every production op needs.

rule.statement: Every model swap ships behind a feature flag with a rollback path measured in <5 minutes and a pre-defined rollback trigger (latency or quality regression threshold).
rule.rationale: Without a trigger the swap stays live through silent regressions; without <5-min rollback the team waits out the regression to avoid the deploy cycle.

rule.statement: A live dashboard shows per-site latency p50/p95 and quality score; an alert fires when any site breaches its SLO for 15 minutes.
rule.rationale: Static grids drift; the dashboard makes the grid load-bearing in operations.

rule.statement: Preconditions hold → execute content/04-procedure.xml and produce grid.json that passes the validator.
Single call site OR no measurable quality signal → skip; build the measurement infra first.
